narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 49.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was the key reason your application was rejected—they prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. Your profile includes a second-decile ranking in Year 1 grades, a first-decile ranking in Year 3 (meaning you were near the bottom of your class those years), a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 27, an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, being male, Asian background, and planning full-time enrollment. Compared to typical students, your LSAT is well below the average of about 37, your undergrad GPA is a bit under the usual 3.21, and your school deciles are much lower than averages around 5-6, while your other traits like gender, race, income, and full-time status align more closely with the majority. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which had the biggest influence and pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which also significantly nudged the outlook toward failure due to how low it was relative to others. Your Year 1 decile came third, reinforcing that early academic struggles carried weight in tipping the scales. Family income in the middle bracket followed, then your Asian racial background (less common in the dataset at about 6% versus 84% White), with your undergrad GPA, gender, and full-time plans having smaller but still failure-pushing effects. Overall, these factors together made the model see higher risk in your bar passage compared to stronger applicants whose profiles pulled more toward success. While some elements like race or gender aren't changeable, you can realistically retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above the average, which could dramatically shift future predictions since it's a top influencer. Strengthening future academic deciles through better study habits or coursework would also help, as your school performance was the leading concern. Building a stronger undergrad record if possible, or highlighting improvements since then, might address the GPA dip. Consider reapplying after these steps—many students boost their profiles this way and get approved later. Hang in there; this is a data-driven call, not a judgment on your potential.
